Notes:
Overall: These are essentially blog posts. Not sure we need both this section AND a blog. "Ask A" and "Blog" might instead be combined under Blog or under a content-based "what's new" sort of category. If keeping, there are several sub-sections that should be renamed and several posts that are outdated and should be deleted.

Specific Notes:
- There are subsections of content within this category: Classroom Management, Collaboration, Collecting and Organizing, Multimedia, Presentations, Research, Social Networking, Visuals and Visualizing. Note- many posts are cross-listed in multiple subsections.
- These subsections are unclear and posts are often cross-posted in several. I don't know that we need subsections if the titles are clear enough but if we keep subsections, they should certainly be renamed/formatted.
- several of the posts are outdated and will need to be deleted or revised.

Overall: These are also essentially blog posts and there are FAR more of them. Basically historians are responding to specific historical questions. They are outdated but some may remain useful after review/updates.

Specific Notes:
- Some of these will want to be read closely for edits, some will likely need to be deleted. We might also consider deleting some just because there are SO many (174) and were only written between 2008 and 2012.
- That said, these may be useful for whatever blog feature we retain.
- I do think it would be helpful to perhaps make the title of the post the question being answered because it is not always obvious.
- The content can be search by key words, topics and time periods. This seems adequete.

Overall: The number of posts in this category is between that for digital historians and historians. These are also responses to questions, but these tend to be questions about how to teach specific historical content. They are also very outdated and in need of review/deletion.
Specific Notes:
- IF we keep the "Ask A" format rather than a "what's new" type of thing, it should not say "Master Educator" but rather be changed to "Educator."
- There are subsections within this category (like "ask a digital historian" and unlike "ask a historian") that are not probably helpful.  These categories are not visible until one clicks on an individual post. These subsections should be deleted or reworked.
- a good number of these are probably useful still because they speak to non-tech specific pedagogical issues/solutions BUT should be read/reviewed and I would recommend deleting a portion of them so the selection is not overwhelming.
- again recommend clearer and consistent titles.
- This material can be searched via keywords. Some additional categorization might be helpful to include in searching if this is kept as a seperate category.

Overall: There is no signposting what exactly this category of content is/why it is useful. Each page is a video, some with transcrtiptions, that are all outdated and should be reviewed, some, if not all, deleted. This category should either absorb other content categories on the site or be absorbed into another category.

Specific Notes:
- There are 15 total "Beyond the Chalkboard" posts/pages.
- A lot of this content overlaps with content in the "Ask A" section. Example: whiteboard usage, skype, social media.
- consider deleting this type of content entirely OR finding new videos and having a videos section OR putting these types of teacher advice videos in the place we decide is for teacher advice.
- Is some of this content related to content under other categories. For example, https://teachinghistory.org/history-content/quiz/25573  and https://teachinghistory.org/digital-classroom/beyond-the-chalkboard/25781 . If so there could be more connectivity between these content areas either using related to/see also.

Overall: There is no signposting what exactly this category of content is/why it is useful. Upon exploration, I found that each post is an essay that discusses how textbooks deal with a particular event/theme in history and uses primary sources to fill out the debate. They should be individually reviewed to see if updates/deletions need to be done. There are some teaching guides that more closely follow this format of how to supplement textbooks.

Specific Notes: 
- 18 total results in this section. Key word searches.
- Do teachers want/use this content? If the answer is yes, then I think these posts accomplish their goals. With review, updates, deletion of some, etc... this section would be fine to keep. It should have a description though that makes it clear that these posts are for teachers looking to supplement textbooks.
- some of the content will likely need to be updated/deleted.
Ideas to Refresh the Content:
- should be titled: Supplementing Textbooks
- may work better as a more practical guide than as just extra reading for teachers. as it is now, there is a main essay with annotated primary sources. I think cutting down on the essay to focus mainly on the primary sources and/or on teaching strategies/tools may be even more effective.
- incorporate more guides on how to get students analyzing primary sources... something inspired by World History Commons?
- guide on how to use podcasts as supplements (with examples)... ex: Humble Servants has discussion questions.

Notes:
Overall: There is 1 post from 2025 but the rest are at least 10 years old. Topics include a wide range of things including news, teaching tools, film reviews, history content.

Specific Notes: 
- 318 total posts.
- This should not exist separately from the "Ask A" section. They are both blog posts. Maybe we create 3 subsections of the blog (teacher, historian, and dh).
- individual posts should be reviewed, updated, and potentially deleted.
- As a user I would not know how to navigate this content or what to expect  when browsing it. Could use some signposting.
Ideas to Refresh the Content:
-  National History Day Guides 
- Topic based guides to explore content 
- Survey - What do you want to see, , how can we improve usability
- Categories that were primarily videos, like examples of historical thinking or teaching in action might be hard to add to with the current budget. An alternative to updating this content could be to have the option for teachers to contribute blog posts. 
- AI Literacy and the Classroom

Notes:
Overall: Very miscellaneous. Links are not external but instead point to thist.org content and further is not comprehensive advice or content specifically for elementary teachers, but rather whatever random content on the site might be relevant. The organization is also confusing/not necessary. IF we keep this category, we either need to create general content to house here OR make it clear that we are linking to random things within the site that may be useful (and link to ALL of those things rather than a few)

Specific Notes: 
- At the top, there is an "Elementary Intro Video" link but it leads nowhere. The video might be the same as the one on the homepage.
- Also at the top there is a "Teacher's Pick" link to a Teaching with Textbooks which is a link to a post titled "Using Historiography to Analyze the Mexican-American War."
- Below this header, there are three main sections: K-2 Resources, 3-5 Resources, and Classroom Ttools. Each of these sections has only two items linked.
       - K-2 Resources: Listed under subheader "Content" is a link to the post "Jennifer Orr on Integrating History into 1st-grade Instruction". Under the subheader "Teaching Tools," is a link to the Beyond the Chalkboard video "Whiteboards and the Youngest children." First of all, I don't know why we need subheadings under k-2 resources which is itself a subsection. Second, the content may be outdated. Third, we may consider making the content under these sub-sub sections more broad tips/tricks rather than whatever random things on the site that pertain to k-2 teachers.
       - 3-5 Resources: Listed under subheader "Pre-1865," is a link to a lesson plan "Declaration of Independence: Rough Draft to Proclamation." Under subheader "Post-1865" is a link to a website review "Laura Jernegan: Girl on a Whaleship." Again, I don't think we need sub-sub sections, but if we do having them be Pre and Post 1865 does not make sense.
       - Classroom Tools: Listed under subheader "Historical Thinking & Literacy" is a link to teaching guide "Applying KWL Guides to Sources with Elementary Students." Under subheader "Using Technology" is a link to a website review "The Pilgrims in American Culture: Thanksgiving." We do not need these sub-sub sections and also not sure the second link is even relevant.
- None of the links lead to any content from Best Practices.
Ideas to Refresh the Content:
-Adding more content, rotating content, switching to a model where this highlights a few resources and provides links to search features or other pages that will help you find more resources by grade level. 
- Alternatively, instead of it being rotating content, this page could just bring up every post/page on the site that is "tagged" for elementary school level. This way it would bring up all relevant content at all times.
- write about some of the ideas in this post: https://truthforteachers.com/social-studies/
- See, Think, Wonder format with primary source examples. https://www.facinghistory.org/resource-library/see-think-wonder
- Summarize/Review/Highlight this podcast episode: "A Case for Teaching History in Elementary School". https://open.spotify.com/episode/4NHdkOAIb79xw64HcOXPyI
- Content from this guide would be helpful. Could highlight this guide overall, or review/explain it in pieces. https://www.socialstudies.org/sites/default/files/publications/inquiry-ebook/Chapter4.pdf

Notes:
Overall: This is almost perfect. Most everything is clearly organized, signposted, explained, and relevant. Besides making sure everything works/is up to date, the only thing that should be changed is collapsing 2 of the link sections at the bottom of the page into one since they are so similar and will be used similarly by teachers.

Specific Notes: 
- The Introduction is great. Within a few short sentences, it clearly states the difficulty of ELL, the opportunity history instruction offers, and introduces that a person has reviewed research to create 3 guidelines (plus resources) for ELL teachers.
- The three guidelines each have a brief description.
- There is an "In the First Language" section with links to translated content on the site or posts describing how to translate content. This is great. should just add new things/review for relevance.
- There is a "From the Unversity" section at the bottom that includes links to various advice posts about various teaching things AND a "From the Classroom" section that has very similar content. These sections should be combined and also each post should be reviewed to make sure it's up to date/still relevant and functioning.
Ideas to Refresh the Content:
- Since this page is so organized and straight forward and not too out of date, there's not much we need to change.
- Have an ELL teacher review the content to see if it's problematic or could be updated.
- Could add content from these sources: 
https://www.edutopia.org/article/teaching-history-high-school-ells/
https://www.colorincolorado.org/sites/default/files/ELL-Social-Studies-History-Tips.pdf
https://www.colorincolorado.org/teaching-ells/ell-classroom-strategy-library/teaching-map-skills
https://www.colorincolorado.org/article/primary-sources-library-congress-and-english-learners
https://coffeehousehistorian.com/2024/09/01/teaching-historical-writing-to-english-language-learners/
https://www.socialstudies.org/system/files/publications/articles/se_7306271.pdf
- A reddit thread said that there are good leveled materials for different lexile levels on the OER project... might be worth looking into even outside of this section: https://www.oerproject.com/World-History

Notes:
Overall: 
The concept is okay as it provides an additional way to access material on the website, however, the way it is used is not very helpful way. Overview:
1 Item
This page is accessible from the footer, and the home page. 
Much like the Elementary and High School versions, this is a very miscellaneous guide that compiles together content from the site that references or may in some way be useful to middle schoool teachers. The resources are divided into two sections, 6-8 Resources (Pre 1865 and Post 1865) and Classroom Tools (Historical thinking & Literacy and Using Technology). It spotlights  ab0ut 7 resource. It does not have any broad content about teaching Middle School History for example (A guide, some more introductory materials). I think if we keep this model of highlighting resources by school age, we should include some more generalized content and/or highlight more than just a few random things from the site.

Specific Notes: 
The video "Middle School Intro Video" is not accessible by clicking on the link on the page but if it is the same resource on the homepage it might be accessible from the homepage.
Two of the linked pages are no longer on the website. The featured resources fall under the content areas of History Content, Best Practices, and Blog. None of the featured resources highlight Teaching Materials or Digital Classrooms. 
The concept is good but the execution is flawed, it provides an additional way to access materials and links content across the categories. There should be more guides like this. However, it does not look like the resoures rotate or that there is an option to access more than the six resources featured so I think the potental is limited. It is also not clear how or why these resources where chosen nor why placed into these categories.
This page could be combined or featuerd on the Blog/ask a section. 
Some of the content includes a grade level search filter it might also be helpful to include this search filter when searching across the website or the larger content areas.
Ideas to Refresh the Content: - Same as Quick links for High school
